coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn-on chemosensors based on surface-functionalized MoS2 quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Ta-Wei
Dhenadhayalan, Namasivayam
Lee, Hsin-Lung
Lin, Yih-Tyng
Lin, King-Chuen
Chang, A.H.H.
coredata.dc:description: The multiple sensing capabilities of molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) towards metal ions were scrutinized by tuning their surface functional groups. The MoS2 QDs surface was individually modified with thiol-containing capping agents to form carboxylic-, amine- and thiol-functionalized MoS2 QDs (MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH) by the facile hydrothermal method. Each as-prepared QDs exhibits strong excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior. The design of MoS2 QDs based metal ion sensor was implemented based on the fluorescence turn-on mechanism. These MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs sensors exhibit superior performance towards the highly selective detection of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions, respectively, due to the varied association of each functional group towards metal ions. The resultant detection limit of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ was evaluated to be 54.5, 99.6 and 0.84 nM, respectively, and the related fluorescence turn-on mechanism is verified unambiguously. The binding energies were calculated for QDs with metal ions pairs and the results lent support to the determined sensitivity. The as-prepared QDs were also successfully demonstrated to detect the above metal ions in real water samples. While becoming potential candidates in the chemosensors based on the fluorescence probe, these surface modified MoS2 QDs can offer an excellent sensing capability for specific metal ions with extremely high selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Transition metal dichalcogenides
MoS2QDs
Metal-ion sensor
Fluorescence
Turn-on
Surface functionalization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of metal ions in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for sensing mechanism of metal ions.
HRTEM images and particle size distributions of (a, d) MoS2/COOH, (b, e) MoS2/NH2 and (c, f) MoS2/SH QDs.
XRD patterns and FTIR spectra of (a, b) MoS2/COOH, (c, d) MoS2/NH2 and (e, f) MoS2/SH QDs.
XPS spectra of (a, b, c) MoS2/COOH, (d, e, f) MoS2/NH2 and (g, h) MoS2/SH QDs.
Selectivity plot of (a) MoS2/COOH, (b) MoS2/NH2 and (c) MoS2/SH QDs in the presence of diverse metal ions.
Fluorescence spectra and intensity response of (a, b) MoS2/COOH, (c, d) MoS2/NH2 and (e, f) MoS2/SH QDs with varying concentration of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions, respectively.
Binding energies for QD/M2+ ion pairs calculated by using the B3LYP/LanL2DZ method.
Comparison of various fluorescent sensors for Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions detection.
Detection of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn-on chemosensors based on surface-functionalized MoS2 quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10617, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Atomic and Molecular Sciences, Academia Sinica, Taipei 10617, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Atomic and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Dong Hwa University, Shoufeng, Hualien 974, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10617, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The multiple sensing capabilities of molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) towards metal ions were scrutinized by tuning their surface functional groups. The MoS2 QDs surface was individually modified with thiol-containing capping agents to form carboxylic-, amine- and thiol-functionalized MoS2 QDs (MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH) by the facile hydrothermal method. Each as-prepared QDs exhibits strong excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior. The design of MoS2 QDs based metal ion sensor was implemented based on the fluorescence turn-on mechanism. These MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs sensors exhibit superior performance towards the highly selective detection of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions, respectively, due to the varied association of each functional group towards metal ions. The resultant detection limit of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ was evaluated to be 54.5, 99.6 and 0.84 nM, respectively, and the related fluorescence turn-on mechanism is verified unambiguously. The binding energies were calculated for QDs with metal ions pairs and the results lent support to the determined sensitivity. The as-prepared QDs were also successfully demonstrated to detect the above metal ions in real water samples. While becoming potential candidates in the chemosensors based on the fluorescence probe, these surface modified MoS2 QDs can offer an excellent sensing capability for specific metal ions with extremely high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the two dimensional (2D) molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) material has been extensively used for various applications such as sensors, energy storage, catalysis, hydrogen evolution and optoelectronics [1–11]. The exfoliation process of bulk MoS2 material may possibly form defect sites due to the sulfur vacancies of MoS2 on the surface [12–22]. By taking advantage of such a defect, the 2D MoS2 is apt to be chemically functionalized. The sulfur vacancy provides an appropriate site for surface modification with small molecules including diazonium salts, organoiodides, dibenzothiphene, thionine and thiol containing molecules [12–22]. As reported, Makarova et al. have examined the interaction between MoS2 and thiol molecules in which the thiol groups should bind with the Mo atoms at the sulfur vacancy defects [23]. Cho et al. have further investigated the functionalization of MoS2 atomic crystals with alkanethiol molecules confirming the thiol end groups in alkanethiols form covalent bonds with Mo atoms at vacancy sites [17].

The MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) in the family of transition metal dichalcogenides (TMD) nanomaterials is nowadays attracting wide attention as a fluorescent probe in chemo- and bio-sensors, bioimaging, and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [24–30]. For instance, Dong et al. have applied fluorescent MoS2 QDs to look into the bioimaging and PDT behavior [28]. Likewise, Dai et al. reported MoS2 QDs in intracellular microRNA detection and multiphoton bioimaging [30]. Due to its strong fluorescence, photostability, and low toxicity, MoS2 QDs based fluorescence sensors have been reported for the detection of nitro explosives (2,4,6-trinitrophenol [31,32] and 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene [33]), biomolecules (BSA [34], glutathione [35], hyaluronidase [24] and dopamine [36]), pesticide (methyl parathion [37]), and several chemical compounds including glucose [38], hydrogen peroxide [38,39], and hypochlorite [40]. Thus far, very few articles are recently published related to MoS2 QDs based metal ion sensor [41–44]. Recently, Wu et al. have reported the fluorescence sensing of Al3+ and Fe3+ metal ions by cysteine functionalized MoS2 QDs [41]. Similarly, Li et al. utilized MoS2 QDs as a sensor for detection of Cu2+ based on the fluorescence quenching effect due to excited electron transfer between the MoS2 QDs and Cu2+ [42]. This work aims to develop the MoS2 QDs based chemosensors for metal ion detection, in which surface functional group is tuned with a different capping agent. As the capping agents, alkyl thiols with different functional groups (COOH, NH2, and SH) are used to make different nature of functionalized MoS2 QDs. Further, we carry out theoretical calculations to evaluate the binding energy between functional MoS2 and metal ions selected. By combining both experimental examination and theoretical prediction, the sensitivity and selectivity for the metal ion detection can be reasonably interpreted. To the best of our knowledge, it is for the first time to take into account both experiment and theory for functionalized MoS2 QDs in the chemical sensing application.

Herein, we have synthesized MoS2 QDs from corresponding nanosheets by the facile hydrothermal method. Then, the surface of QDs was modified individually with a capping agent including thioglycolic acid (TGA), cysteamine (CYS), and 1,3-propanedithiol (PDT) to form carboxylic-, amine- and thiol-functionalized MoS2 QDs (MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2, and MoS2/SH), respectively. The thiol group in the capping agents could be coordinated with QDs through the interaction of metal–sulfur such that the carboxylic-, amine- and thiol-functional groups are projected outward on the surface. The distinct nature of functional groups is expected to achieve multiple metal ion sensing in which each functionalized QDs can sense diverse metal ions using fluorescence titration method. Here, we report the MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2, and MoS2/SH QDs based metal ion sensor towards the sensing of Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ ions, respectively. Each sensor shows an excellent selectivity with high sensitivity in the detection of metal ions. Moreover, these sensors were applied in real water samples to demonstrate the practical applicability. Because the Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ have been widely used in industries (such as batteries, pigments, metallurgy, etc.), pharmaceutical, and agriculture fields [45–48]. These sources can easily immerse these elements into the areas of water which are not biodegradable and thereby possible to contaminate the water and food. These elements especially for Pb2+ and Cd2+ are recognized as most toxic substances to the environment, humans and animals according to standard limit reported by world health organization (WHO) and the agency for toxic substances and disease registry (ATSDR) [49]. The toxicity of Cd2+ will severely harm human beings including heart disease, and cancer as well as affect the functioning of kidney, lung and liver [45–47]. In the case of Pb2+ even in the presence of very low exposure, it can mainly damage the brain functioning and nervous system [45–47]. The Co2+ is less toxic compared to Cd2+ and Pb2+, and a trace uptake is necessitated for human health, but the excess accumulation inversely causes toxic effects on the humans including heart disease and neurological disorder [48]. Due to the toxicity of heavy metal ions, it should be urgent to fabricate an advanced material to detect these metal ions with high sensitivity, and of great importance to quantify them in environmental water samples.

MoS2 powder material was purchased from Acros Organics. TGA, CYS and PDT were purchased from Alfa Aesar and Acros. HEPES buffer solution (1 M) was purchased from Gibco by Life technologies. All commercially available organic reagents were used without further purification unless otherwise stated.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was conducted with a Philips Tecnai F20 G2 FEI-TEM. Wide-angle X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of QDs were recorded on a PANalytical (X'Pert PRO) diffractometer. Infrared spectra of QDs were recorded with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed using VG scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (VG Scientific, UK). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded with a Bruker AVIII HD 400 MHz NMR spectrometer. The absorption spectra of QDs were acquired with a Thermo Scientific evolution 220 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra of QDs were recorded with a Perkin-Elmer LS45 spectrometer. All the measurements were carried out at ambient temperature.

A simple sonication and hydrothermal methods were employed to synthesis of MoS2 QDs from bulk material [50]. Concisely, MoS2 powder (0.5 g) was dissolved in 150 mL of N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent followed by sonication for 20 h at 200 W power to obtain nanosheets (NSs) solution [51]. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to remove bulk residues. The supernatant was collected for preparation of QDs. The MoS2 NSs solution was heated at 180 °C for 8 h under reflux condition. The solution then changed colour while heating, indicating the formation of QDs. The obtained solution was cooled down to room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Then the NMP solvent was removed from the collected supernatant by vacuum distillation. The obtained QDs was washed well with ample amount of Millipore water and dried by rotary evaporator to afford NMP-free MoS2 QDs. Finally as-prepared MoS2 QDs (1 μg/mL) was dissolved in Millipore water for further surface modifications.

To synthesize the MoS2/COOH QDs, TGA (100 mM) was added into 25 mL of aqueous solution of MoS2 QDs. The reaction mixture was refluxed for 12 h. After the reaction, the obtained product was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 15 min) to remove large size particles. The supernatant was purified by using gel PD-10 column (GE Healthcare) to separate unreacted TGA and non-fluorescent particles. The solution was then filtered through the column to collect each fraction which was visualized upon 365 nm UV light. The fluorescent fractions were selected by comparing their fluorescence. The resultant sample was further purified with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) membrane-based centrifugal filter of 3 and 10 K. Finally, the pure MoS2/COOH QDs was concentrated and dissolved in pH 7.4 HEPES buffer for further characterizations and sensing experiments.

By analogy, the amine- and thiol-functionalized MoS2 QDs were prepared according to the above-mentioned procedures in which CYS (100 mM) and PDT (100 mM) were used individually to prepare amine- and thiol-functionalized MoS2 QDs, respectively. But in the case of thiol-functionalized MoS2 QDs, PDT was dissolved in isopropanol instead of aqueous solution due to the insoluble nature of PDT in water.

The fluorescence sensing of metal ions was conducted by monitoring changes in the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared MoS2 QDs upon addition of respective metal ions. The HEPES buffer solution (10 mM) was prepared at pH 7.4. The stock solutions of all MoS2 QDs were made by diluting respective concentrated QDs with HEPES until an appropriate absorbance value (A400 nm at ∼0.1) was reached. The metal ions solutions were prepared in HEPES buffer solution. The fluorescence titration method was used to determine the sensitivity of metal ions. In a typical assay, the Co2+ solution (up to 30 μL, and the maximum concentration of Co2+ in solution is 150 μM) was added into the MoS2/COOH solution in a quartz cuvette. The solution was mixed well with each added Co2+ prior to recording the fluorescence spectrum. A similar procedure was followed for MoS2/NH2 with Cd2+ and MoS2/SH with Pb2+ sensor systems. The sensitivity was calculated based on the changes in the fluorescence intensity with the addition of respective metal ions.

For the selectivity experiments, various metal ions (10 μM for MoS2/COOH and MoS2/NH2; 40 nM for MoS2/SH) with the same concentration was added individually to a set of each QDs solution. After the solutions were mixed well, the fluorescence spectra were monitored at the excitation of 400 nm to find out the selectivity extent for metal ions. As for the experiment of matrix effect, different metal ions (10 μM) were added to the set of a mixture solution of MoS2/COOH and Co2+ (10 μM), and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation of 400 nm. Likewise, the same matrix solutions were prepared for the examination of MoS2/NH2 + Cd2+ (10 μM) and MoS2/SH + Pb2+ (40 nM) sensor systems.

Density functional B3LYP with basis set LanL2DZ calculations were carried out for QD/M2+ ion pairs in water. The energy and harmonic frequencies of optimized geometry of QDs are obtained, and likewise the energies of metal ions. The zero of the energies for the QD/M2+ system is set at asymptotic limit. The energies for optimized bound QD/M2+ species relative to the corresponding asymptotic values are then taken as binding energies. GAUSSIAN 16 programs [52] are employed in electronic structure calculations.

Tap water and pond water samples were collected from our university campus. All water samples were filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane (Millipore) to remove impurities, then, the filtrate samples were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min. The stock solutions of all MoS2 QDs were prepared by the dilution of respective concentrated QDs with each water samples, and fixed the absorbance value (A400 nm at ∼0.1). The fluorescence spectra of each QDs in water samples were measured after addition of each concentration of metal ions.

The MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs were synthesized successfully by hydrothermal synthesis method. The preparation of surface modified MoS2 QDs involves three step processes as follows: (1) preparation of MoS2 nanosheets from their bulk material, (2) preparation of MoS2 QDs from their nanosheets, and (3) surface modification of MoS2 QDs. All the as-prepared QDs were characterized by HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, and NMR measurements. The obtained characterization results for bare MoS2 QDs shown in Fig. S1 and for functionalized MoS2 QDs are shown in Figs. 1–3
and S2-S4. The HRTEM images show that all QDs nanospheres are uniformly distributed (Fig. 1). Particle size distribution indicates the size ranges from ∼2 to 8 nm with an average of ∼4.4 nm for all MoS2 QDs. The crystal structure of functionalized QDs was recognized by XRD as referred to bulk MoS2 (Fig. 2a,c,e). The XRD pattern of bulk MoS2 exhibits intense diffraction peaks at ∼14.4, 28.5, 43.6 and 59.7° corresponding to the (002), (004), (006) and (008) planes, respectively, which are indexed to the 2H phase of MoS2 [53,54]. The obtained XRD patterns for all MoS2 QDs are nearly identical to each other. Interestingly, the intensities of diffraction peaks for all MoS2 QDs were found to decrease as compared to bulk MoS2. The diffraction peak of MoS2 at ∼14° characterizes the d-spacing between the layers of crystals, while the peak intensity depends on the thickness of layers. For instance, the bulk material shows the peak at ∼14° with high intensity, whereas the monolayer materials show much weaker intensity or no peak at that position. The QDs, which are smaller in size compared to nanosheets, probably possess monolayers due to the feasible exfoliation of nanosheets by the solvothermal treatment. Consequently, the QDs have almost no interaction between each layers. The bulk material consists of a great number of layers, thereby showing the strong intensity peak. In the present work, the diffraction peak at 14.4° was not found for all MoS2 QDs; the result was consistent with those reported previously [24,29,55]. Xu et al. have observed the weak peak at 14.4° for MoS2 nanosheets, whereas no peak was observed for MoS2 QDs [56]. This observation strongly supports the formation of quantum dots with smaller sized structure, which is also well reflected in the HRTEM results. The diffraction peaks observed at 32.3, 35.3 and 49.4° in all MoS2 QDs are indexed to the (100), (102) and (105) planes, respectively [24,56].

The FTIR spectra of all as-prepared MoS2 QDs were recorded to confirm the presence of functional groups on the surface of QDs. The Fig. 2b,d,f shows the resultant FTIR spectra of functionalized QDs accompanied by those of respective functional agents (TGA, CYS and PDT). The non-functionalized MoS2 QDs do not have any specific bands. The FTIR spectra for TGA, CYS and PDT show prominent bands especially at ∼2569 cm−1 corresponding to the SH vibration [13,22,57]. Note that the SH band disappeared at 2569 cm-1 for all functionalized MoS2 QDs. This results confirm that the thiol group in TGA, CYS and PDT is covalently bound on the surface of MoS2 QDs through sulfur (thiol) – metal bonds. The FTIR spectrum of MoS2/COOH QDs exhibits bands at ∼1710 and 3530 cm-1due to the stretching vibration of CO and OH band, respectively [58]; the strong peaks of carboxylic groups appearing at the spectral position identical to TGA suggest that the carboxylic groups should be projected outward on the surface without any hindrance (Fig. 2b). Similarly, the FTIR spectrum of MoS2/NH2 QDs shows bands at ∼1125, 1605 and 3400 cm-1 corresponding to the amine CN stretching, NH bending and stretching vibration of amine groups, respectively [58], thereby confirming the existence of the amine groups on the surface of MoS2/NH2 QDs (Fig. 2d).

XPS measurements were carried out for further structural analysis of functionalized QDs. As shown in Fig. 3a,d,g, the XPS spectra exhibit two characteristic peaks of Mo at ∼228.5 and 234 eV corresponding to Mo 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively [24,26,28,30], while the peaks observed at ∼162.5 and 164 eV are ascribed to S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 3b,e,h) [24,26,28,30]. These findings confirm that Mo and S exist in the Mo4+ and S2− oxidation state. Moreover, XPS results reveal a 2H phase in the MoS2 crystal structure, which is consistent with the XRD results [26,28,30]. The COOH, NH2 and SH groups on the surface of MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs were confirmed by the observation of related XPS peaks in their respective QDs. As shown in Fig. 3c, the peaks at 533.8 and 532.4 eV corresponding to organic CO and CO were observed for O in MoS2/COOH QDs. Similarly, the presence of C-NH2 group in MoS2/NH2 QDs was identified due to the peak observed at 400.6 eV (Fig. 3f). Note that in Fig. 3h, the XPS spectrum of S for MoS2/SH QDs exhibits an additional peak at 167.1 eV which is assigned to the -SH group.

In addition, the functionalized MoS2 QDs were characterized by 1H NMR measurements and the obtained results are shown in Figs. S2-S4. The NMR spectrum of TGA shows the characteristic proton peak of SH group and OH for carboxylic acid at 10.6 and 2.2 ppm, respectively (Fig. S2). On the other hand, the respective proton peak of SH group disappears in MoS2/COOH QDs, revealing the interaction of SH group of capping agent with QDs, while observation of proton peak for −OH confirms the presence of COOH group on the surface of QDs. As shown in Fig. S3, the NMR spectrum of MoS2/NH2 QDs exhibits the proton peaks at 2.6–3.0 ppm and 8.3 ppm corresponding to the methylene groups and NH2 group, respectively, similar to those of amine capping agent (CYS). Note that the SH proton peaks were not found even for CYS capping agent. For the MoS2/SH QDs (Fig. S4), the methylene proton peaks were observed as those of thiol capping agent (PDT) in the NMR spectrum. However, the proton peaks of thiol group for PDT at ∼1.7 ppm couldn’t be observed for MoS2/SH QDs probably due to a poor signal-to-noise. Based on the above HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, and 1H NMR results, it can be concluded that the MoS2 QDs were successfully prepared and further functionalized with each TGA, CYS and PDT to form MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs, respectively.

The absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded for all MoS2 QDs to look into the photophysical properties of QDs. The resultant absorption spectra exhibit higher absorbance in the UV region with a strong shoulder at ∼350 nm (Fig. S5a). The fluorescence spectra of MoS2 QDs were monitored by varying excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. S5b-d. All MoS2 QDs exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent multi-emission behavior which is similar to other reports [24,26,27,29,32]. The fluorescence maximum was shifted to the longer wavelength ranging from ∼450 to 550 nm while varying excitation wavelength from 350 to 500 nm. Though, the higher fluorescence intensity was observed at the excitation of ∼400 nm. The excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence phenomenon may be due to the polydispersity and surface states caused by the presence of surface functional groups [24,26,32,59]. In addition, existence of the different sized particles observed in HRTEM images also leads to the multi-emission of QDs.

These MoS2 QDs are expected to have a superior nature as metal ion sensor due to the presence of surface functional groups as well as strong fluorescence behavior inherent in QDs. In view of these properties, the fluorogenic sensing capability of functionalized MoS2 QDs towards diverse metal ions were investigated for each QDs. The fluorescence titration method conducted at pH 7.4 HEPES-buffer was used to determine the influence of metal ions in the respective QDs fluorescence. These MoS2 QDs sensors were implemented based on the fluorescence turn-on process; however, the selectivity and sensitivity were dependent on the nature of surface functional groups.

To evaluate the selectivity of functionalized QDs towards metal ions, the fluorescence spectra of each functionalized QDs were measured individually in the presence of diverse metal ions including Cr2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+. The resultant selectivity histogram plot is shown in Fig. 4 ; as expected, each functionalized QDs exhibits distinct selectivity towards metal ions. The MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs can selectively sense Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions, respectively, among the metal ions used in this work. It was found that a drastic fluorescence enhancement of each functionalized QDs in the presence of appropriate metal ions. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of each QDs does not vary much in the presence of other metal ions. These results reveal that each functionalized QDs sensor exhibits excellent selectivity in detecting respective metal ions.

Further, the interference effect by other metal ions was studied on the interaction between MoS2/COOH and Co2+, MoS2/NH2 and Cd2+ as well as MoS2/SH and Pb2+. The fluorescence spectra of MoS2/COOH QDs with Co2+ (10 μM) were monitored in the presence of other metal ions. The resultant fluorescence intensities exhibit not much change upon addition of metal ions, as shown in histogram plot of Fig. S6, revealing that there is no significant interference effect on the MoS2/COOH + Co2+ system. Similar results were obtained for MoS2/NH2 + Cd2+ and MoS2/SH + Pb2+ systems (Fig. S6).

When bare MoS2 QDs are applied, the sensing selectivity becomes weak and the possibility for multifunctional probe may be obtained [43,44]. On the other hand, different functional group on MoS2 QDs may sense different species such as cysteine-MoS2 QDs which was used as the sensor for Al3+ and Fe3+ [41]. In our case, MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2, and MoS2/SH QDs are found to be sensitive to Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, respectively. Fe3+ ion is readily detected by cysteine-MoS2 QDs, but appears insensitive to either functionalized MoS2 QDs used by us. In addition, among the Au3+, Cr(VI), and Al3+ ions, the first two ions were sensitive to the bare MoS2 QD [43,44], while the last one was detected with cysteine-functionalized MoS2 QDs [41]. Based on the calculations in this work, the binding energy between functionalized MoS2 (COOH, NH2, and SH) and metal ion may significantly determine the sensitivity and selectivity. It is found that the metal ion interacting directly with the functional group attached by MoS2 minimizes the binding energy, instead of direct interaction with MoS2. That is why cysteine-MoS2 QDs [41], and functionalized MoS2 (COOH, NH2, and SH) QDs appear different sensing capability toward different ion species. The Au3+ and Cr(VI) ions must lower the binding energy to achieve a better LOD while attacking the bare MoS2 QDs. However, neither are the experiments nor the binding energy calculations between functionalized MoS2 and Au3+ (or Cr(VI)) ion conducted in this work such that whether our functionalized MoS2 QDs may be applied to detect Au3+ (or Cr(VI)) ion with a LOD as small as Pb2+, Co2+ and Cd2+ remains uncertain. As for the matrix effect, we expect that the addition of the interfering ions with weak binding energies may result in a small competing capability for the functional groups on MoS2 QDs, and thus these interfering ions may be repelled by the appropriate metal ions (such as Pb2+, Co2+ and Cd2+). That is why there is no significant change observed for the ions added in the selectivity experiment. As for the addition of interfering ions studied, some slightly enhance the fluorescence, whereas others cause the fluorescence quenching slightly. The reasons are yet to know.

The quantitative detection of selective Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ with MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs, respectively, was demonstrated to determine their sensitivity. The corresponding fluorescence spectra of each QDs were measured in the presence of different concentration of metal ions as shown in Fig. 5 . As a result, the fluorescence intensity of each MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs was gradually enhanced with increasing the concentration of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+, respectively. It was found that a drastic fluorescence enhancement was observed at initial concentrations of metal ions while the fluorescence saturation was reached at the higher concentration of metal ions. The detection limit for individual Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions was determined by the corresponding fluorescence as a function of each metal ion using 3(σ/slope) relation where σ is the standard deviation of the response and the slope is obtained from the linear plot [60]. A linear plot was found in the concentration range of 0 to 1 μM for MoS2/COOH + Co2+, 0 to 500 nM for MoS2/NH2 + Cd2+ and 0 to 10 nM for MoS2/SH + Pb2+ sensor systems (Fig. 5). The resulting detection limit of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ was calculated to be 54.5, 99.6 and 0.84 nM for MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs, respectively. Thus, from the above-mentioned results, these sensor systems exhibit excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions.

Based on the fluorescence intensity change of QDs with metal ions, the stoichiometry and association constants were determined for the interaction between QDs and metal ions using the Benesi-Hildebrand method [58,60]. The Benesi-Hildebrand (B–H) plot of 1/(F − F0) as a function of 1/[M2+] exhibits linearity for MoS2/COOH and MoS2/NH2 QDs sensor systems as shown in Fig. S7a,b. Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity at the maximum of QDs in the absence and presence of the metal ions (M2+), respectively. The obtained linearity of this plot indicates a 1:1 stoichiometry; i.e., each single Co2+ and Cd2+ ion could be interacted with one MoS2/COOH and MoS2/NH2, respectively [58,60]. But for the MoS2/SH system, the B–H plot as a function of 1/[M2+] deviates from linearity. In contrast, a plot of 1/(F − F0) as a function of 1/[M2+]2 shows linearity (Fig. S7c), confirming the Pb2+ ions have 1:2 stoichiometry with MoS2/SH QDs that a Pb2+ ion binds with two MoS2/SH QDs [61–63]. In addition, the association constant (Ka) for MoS2/COOH + Co2+, MoS2/NH2+Cd2+ and MoS2/SH + Pb2+ systems were calculated by a ratio of intercept/slope obtained from the B–H plot. Thus, the association constant was found to be 8.5 × 105 M−1, 3.1 × 106 M−1, and 4.6 × 1016 M-2 for MoS2/COOH + Co2+, MoS2/NH2+Cd2+ and MoS2/SH + Pb2+ systems, respectively. This result clearly reveals that these QDs exhibit much stronger binding association with respective metal ions.

The sensing mechanism of metal ions is depicted in Scheme 1 . These MoS2 QDs based metal ion sensors are demonstrated based on fluorescence turn-on mechanism. Upon excitation at 400 nm, the fluorescence occurs at about 480 nm for three surface modified MoS2 QDs, indicating the fluorescence originates from the MoS2 semiconductor band with less influence by the surface modification. This observation indicates that the fluorescence of MoS2 QDs is due to the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs. The bare MoS2 QDs exhibits strong fluorescence because the excitonic recombination between the electron and holes pairs occurs more feasibly with the aid of excess free electrons in the unsaturated Mo atom due to the vacancies of S. The surface functionalization of QDs leads to the bonding formation of capping agents with the S vacancy sites, thus resulting in decrease of excess free electrons. This surface functionalization process diminishes the efficiency of recombination between the electron-hole pairs due to the less free electrons. Consequently, the fluorescence of surface functionalized MoS2 QDs was found to suppress as compared to that of bare MoS2 QDs. Meanwhile, the excited electrons in the conduction band might be delocalized and trapped in the surface states [64]. When appropriate metal ions are added to functionalize MoS2 QDs, the existing d-orbital electrons of metal ions could repel these trapped conduction-band electrons caused by the strong association of surface functional groups with metal ions. This association of metal ions facilitates the recombination of electron-hole pairs and thereby restores the fluorescence intensity of functionalized MoS2 QDs. If the association constant is larger, then the fluorescence is enhanced to a more extent. Thus, the fluorescence of functionalized MoS2 QDs are enhanced upon interaction with appropriate metal ions. For MoS2/COOH QDs, the Co2+ ions have a strong association towards oxygen atoms of carboxylic groups on the QDs surface. Similarly, the Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions could interact with nitrogen atoms of amine groups and sulfur atoms of thiol groups in MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs, respectively. The association of related metal ions on the QDs surface leads to the fluorescence turn-on of each QDs. That is why the fluorescence intensity of each QDs enhances with the metal ion concentration. Moreover, the fluorescence enhancement depends on the binding association between functional groups and metal ion. The detection limit was thus obtained relying on the above-mentioned facts.

From the theoretical point of view, the binding energies were calculated for surface functionalized QDs with respective sensing metal ions to inspect whether the binding energy may play a role to affect the sensitivity. The energy state of each QD/M2+ ion pair was found to be lower in energy than that of QD alone. As shown in Fig. 6 , the binding energies were calculated for two possible forms of QDs including neutral and completely ionized forms. The binding energy for neutral form of MoS2/COOH + Co2+, MoS2/NH2+Cd2+ and 2(MoS2/SH)+Pb2+ pairs was determined to be 16.8, 5.5 and 38.7 kcal/mol, respectively. On the other hand, the corresponding ionized form of QD/M2+ exhibits larger binding energy of about 37.3, 15.7 and 70.7 kcal/mol, respectively. Note that the larger binding energy was found for 2(MoS2/SH)+Pb2+ compared to other pairs. This indicates that the MoS2/SH was more stabilized by the presence of Pb2+ ion. Especially, the ionized form of 2(MoS2-S−)-Pb2+ was further stabilized and resulted in the much lower energy state. Indeed, the functionalized QDs might be partially dissociated in aqueous solution. While taking into account this factor, the trend of binding energy is consistent with the experimental findings in which the Pb2+ probed by MoS2/SH shows the lowest detection limit. Note that the above calculations of binding energies depend on only one functionalized MoS2-M2+ conformer which has been searched by doing our best to represent the most stabilized one, despite the complexity to find out all the conformers. It is not trivial to take into account the entire QD in the ab initio calculations. Instead, we simplify the QD into either a single MoS2 molecule or an H. Despite neglect of a variety of conformers involved, we calculate binding energies for MoS2/COOH + Co2+, MoS2/NH2+Cd2+, and MoS2/SH + Pb2+ and their corresponding ion pairs when dissociated in water, or HCOOH + Co2+, NH3+Cd2+, and SH2+Pb2+ and their ion pairs.

In the calculations of the binding energies of HCOOH-Co2+, NH3-Cd2+ and H2S-Pb2+ and the corresponding ionized forms (Fig. S8), we found 26, 1 and 1 conformers for HCOOH-Co2+, NH3-Cd2+ and H2S-Pb2+ pairs, respectively. Given the average of all of those binding energies by assuming an equal weighting factor for each conformer and adoption of H2O dielectric constant, we obtained 45.7, 17.2 and 18.2 kcal/mol for HCOOH-Co2+, NH3-Cd2+, and SH2-Pb2+, respectively, and 79.1, 60.5 and 71.8 kcal/mol for the corresponding ionized form. The HCOOH-Co2+ energy result differs from that based on a single conformer, indicating the assumption of equal weighting factor for each conformer should be questionable.

It turns out when H is used to replace MoS2 for the interaction calculations, the outcome cannot fit with the trend of the experimental findings. The calculation results highlight the following aspects. Firstly, MoS2 can be used to replace a complicated calculation of MoS2 QDs and reaches a reasonable agreement with the trend of experimental examination. Secondly, MoS2 must be involved in the energy binding calculations. It can’t be replaced with an H. This fact further suggests the unique properties of MoS2. Thirdly, the calculation results suggest that the metal ion sensing should depend on the pH value in the solution. Thus, the pH adjustment may optimize the limit of detection for the metal ions probed. Finally, the binding energy between the functional group and metal ion plays a vital role to determine which metal ion is suitable for selection by the functionalized MoS2 QDs, although it may not be the only key factor for determination. The factors such as proficiency of metal ion coordination, size and charge of the metal ion, nature of functional groups on the material surface (charge, and electron-withdrawing and -donating property), and number of heteroatoms in the surface involved into the binding with metal ion should be taken into account to some extent.

Control experiments were conducted to further support the interaction mechanism between metal ions and surface functional groups. For that, the fluorescence spectra of MoS2 QDs without functionalization were recorded in the presence of each Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions. The resultant fluorescence intensity of QDs did not vary significantly with each metal ions added even at their higher concentration (Fig. S9). These results reveal that the metal ions interact with surface functional groups, instead of QDs core, to cause the fluorescence enhancement of QDs.

When compared to those reported by using fluorescence method, the detection limits for Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ achieved in this work turn out to be comparable to and/or even much better than those sensors as shown in Table 1 [65–82]. Accordingly, the sensing design with functionalized MoS2 QDs is allowed to offer an extremely lower detection limit to the fluorogenic detection of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: For practical application of these sensors, we have monitored the amount of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions contained in pond and tap water. The fluorescence intensity change of respective MoS2 QDs was acquired with respect to the different concentrations of metal ions. All water samples were purified by the processes of filtration and centrifugation. Then, addition of each MoS2 QDs in each water samples did not yield much difference in the fluorescence intensity of MoS2 QDs compared with those in HEPES. This indicates that these water samples do not contain Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions. Then, the different concentrations of each Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions were added in both water samples. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of each QDs increases with increasing concentration of metal ions. These results clearly reveal that the MoS2 QDs fluorescent sensors possess excellent sensing capability of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions in real water samples. The average recovery of metal ions and relative standard deviations (RSD) for three replicates of experiments are listed in Table 2 .

The sensing of different metal ions was investigated by tuning the surface functional groups of MoS2 QDs. The surface functionalized MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs were successfully synthesized and well characterized. The formed QDs possess ∼4.4 nm diameter in size and the surface functional groups in each QDs were confirmed by FTIR. The fluorescence sensing capability of each MoS2/COOH, MoS2/NH2 and MoS2/SH QDs was investigated showing excellent selectivity and sensitivity in the detection of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ ions, respectively. These sensor systems were implemented based on fluorescence turn-on mechanism with appropriate metal ions involved. The fluorescence intensity of QDs was increased with increasing concentration of metal ions. These MoS2 QDs sensors exhibit superior performance towards the detection of Co2+, Cd2+ and Pb2+ metal ions with the detection limit of 54.5, 99.6 and 0.84 nM, respectively. The strong association of each metal ions with surface functional groups brings about the detection limit as low as in the nanomolar range. Furthermore, these sensors were demonstrated to detect these metal ions in real water samples for practical applications with satisfactory recovery efficiency. As a result, these QDs synthesized could offer a promising design of chemosensor based on the fluorescent probe.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan, Republic of China, under the contract number NSC 102-2113-M-002-009-MY3. N.D. wishes to thank Academia Sinica for a post-doctoral fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Characterizations of MoS2 QDs; the absorption and fluorescence spectra of functionalized MoS2 QDs; fluorescence response of QDs/metal ion systems with diverse metal ions; Benesi-Hildebrand plot; binding energies for ion pairs; fluorescence spectra of MoS2 QDs with varying concentrations of metal ions.
